Several caveats apply to the present analysis. First, the projections hold ex-vessel prices and management rules constant at historical levels, implying that the long-run responses capture the mechanical effect of changed environmental and biological conditions without accounting for endogenous price adjustments or adaptive management. @sumaila2011 note that reduced fish supply under climate change could increase prices, partially offsetting revenue losses from lower catches, while @Lam2016 project sizable changes in global fisheries revenues by the 2050s once price responses and cross-country trade are accounted for. Incorporating an inverse demand system and numerical optimization of quota paths is a natural extension to capture these equilibrium adjustments. Second, the use of chlorophyll-a projections from a single Earth system model introduces additional uncertainty, as the direction and magnitude of primary productivity changes under climate change remain contested across models [@sumaila2011]. Third, the shifters are identified on a 25-year time series and, while the state-space specification propagates process-noise and measurement-noise uncertainty into the posterior, its ability to detect non-linearities in the climate response is limited. Fourth, our models do not explicitly incorporate risk preferences or production risk, which may play a role in shaping fleet-level responses to environmental variability [@Kasperski2013-jz; @Sethi2014-bn]. Fifth, the biological-closure variable in the trip equation currently varies only across regulatory zones and therefore captures locational heterogeneity rather than the causal effect of within-vessel exposure to closed days. Incorporating year-specific closure dates from annual SUBPESCA resolutions would introduce within-zone temporal variation and is a planned extension.

The finding that climate change creates winners and losers within the same fishery has direct implications for quota allocation policy. Chile's current system allocates quotas separately for the artisanal and industrial sectors, with limited transferability across fleet segments. If the long-run productivity of the coastal-upwelling pair declines by the magnitudes reported in Table~\ref{tab:growth_compstat}, the relative value of artisanal quotas---heavily concentrated in those stocks---could fall substantially, potentially motivating reforms to the quota allocation system to allow more flexible cross-sector transfers. This echoes the portfolio literature on fishery diversification [@Kasperski2013-jz], which shows that access to a broader species portfolio reduces revenue variability and therefore that secure, transferable rights across fleet segments would facilitate adaptation to climate-driven shifts in species composition.

# Conclusions

This paper estimates a multi-species bioeconomic model for Chile's small pelagic fishery and quantifies the long-run productivity response of each stock to CMIP6 climate scenarios. We contribute to the literature in three ways. First, we identify---rather than merely estimate---a set of structural climate shifters $(\rho_i^{SST}, \rho_i^{CHL})$ that modulate the intrinsic growth rate of each stock in a Bayesian state-space specification calibrated on official IFOP and SPRFMO assessments, and we demonstrate that the resulting parameters are economically interpretable as semi-elasticities of stock productivity with respect to thermal and primary-productivity anomalies. Second, we evaluate these shifters under CMIP6 projections (IPSL-CM6A-LR, SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5) and report their comparative-statics implications for intrinsic productivity together with the full posterior of the response. Third, we link the identified structural response to the negative binomial trip equations estimated separately for the artisanal and industrial fleets, and we show that the direction of the distributional asymmetry across fleets is set by the differential exposure of each fleet's species portfolio to the identified shifters---not by reduced-form correlations that a forecasting-oriented specification would deliver.

Our projections indicate that the long-run productivity of the coastal-upwelling pair---anchoveta and sardina común---is poised to decline sharply under all CMIP6 scenarios considered, with sardina común consistently more exposed than anchoveta and with the SSP5-8.5 end-of-century scenario essentially eliminating the intrinsic productivity of sardina común at the Centro-Sur scale. The Centro-Sur jack mackerel stock, by contrast, does not provide sufficient information to identify a local climate response in the 2000--2024 sample, consistent with its transboundary SPRFMO management. These results suggest that management policies focused on aggregate effort levels may miss important heterogeneity across fleets and species, and that climate adaptation in this fishery will need to address the uneven distribution of impacts through the quota-allocation architecture rather than through operational margins.

Several extensions to the present framework are natural. A first extension is to incorporate trip-level cost functions and an inverse demand system to enable full numerical optimization of quota paths under climate change, following the complete @Kasperski2015-jm approach. The spatial dimension of effort allocation is also a natural extension, connecting the multi-species model to the location choice literature [e.g., @Dupont1993-jn; @Smith2005-us; @Hicks2020-mz], since the geographic distribution of species availability is likely to shift under warming. Finally, incorporating risk preferences and production risk---through, for example, random coefficient models---would provide a richer characterization of how fishers respond to the increased environmental variability projected under climate change [@Kasperski2013-jz].

# Repository

The source code for this project is available on
[GitHub](https://github.com/fquezadae/Impact-of-Environmental-Variability-on-Harvest)


# References

<div id="refs"></div>


# (APPENDIX) Appendix {-}

# Additional robustness checks {#appendix-robustness}

```{r SUR_rob_panel_A, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, results='asis'}

tex_rob1 <- capture.output(
  stargazer(
    res_rob1_r[[1]]$lm, res_rob1_r[[2]]$lm, res_rob1_r[[3]]$lm,
    type = "latex",
    header = FALSE,
    float = TRUE,
    title = paste0("SUR robustness: observed jurel CS only (N = ", nrow(sur_obs), ")"),
    label = "tab:SUR_rob_obs",
    column.labels = c("Sardine", "Anchoveta", "Jack mackerel"),
    column.separate = c(1, 1, 1),
    dep.var.labels.include = FALSE,
    covariate.labels = c(
      "Own biomass ($x_{it}$)",
      "Own biomass$^2$ ($x_{it}^2$)",
      "SST",
      "Chlorophyll-a",
      "Constant"
    ),
    se = list(res_rob1_r[[1]]$se, res_rob1_r[[2]]$se, res_rob1_r[[3]]$se),
    digits = 3,
    single.row = TRUE,
    omit.stat = c("f"),
    no.space = TRUE,
    font.size = "footnotesize",
    notes = c("$^{*}$p$<$0.1; $^{**}$p$<$0.05; $^{***}$p$<$0.01. Robust SEs.",
              "Only years with observed (non-interpolated) jurel CS biomass.",
              "SST$^2$ dropped for parsimony given reduced sample."),
    notes.append = FALSE,
    notes.align = "l"
  )
)
cat(fix_stargazer_notes(tex_rob1), sep = "\n")

```



```{r SUR_rob_panel_B, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, results='asis'}

tex_rob2 <- capture.output(
  stargazer(
    res_rob2_r[[1]]$lm, res_rob2_r[[2]]$lm,
    type = "latex",
    header = FALSE,
    float = TRUE,
    title = paste0("SUR robustness: two-species, jurel as exogenous (N = ", nrow(sur_2sp), ")"),
    label = "tab:SUR_rob_2sp",
    column.labels = c("Sardine", "Anchoveta"),
    column.separate = c(1, 1),
    dep.var.labels.include = FALSE,
    covariate.labels = c(
      "Own biomass ($x_{it}$)",
      "Own biomass$^2$ ($x_{it}^2$)",
      "SST",
      "SST$^2$",
      "Chlorophyll-a",
      "Jack mackerel biomass",
      "Constant"
    ),
    se = list(res_rob2_r[[1]]$se, res_rob2_r[[2]]$se),
    digits = 3,
    single.row = TRUE,
    omit.stat = c("f"),
    no.space = TRUE,
    font.size = "footnotesize",
    notes = c("$^{*}$p$<$0.1; $^{**}$p$<$0.05; $^{***}$p$<$0.01. Robust SEs.",
              "Two-species SUR; jack mackerel biomass enters as exogenous covariate."),
    notes.append = FALSE,
    notes.align = "l"
  )
)
cat(fix_stargazer_notes(tex_rob2), sep = "\n")

```


```{r SUR_rob_interactions, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, results='asis'}

tex_rob3 <- capture.output(
  stargazer(
    res_rob3_r[[1]]$lm, res_rob3_r[[2]]$lm, res_rob3_r[[3]]$lm,
    type = "latex",
    header = FALSE,
    float = TRUE,
    title = paste0("SUR robustness: with cross-species interactions (N = ", nrow(sur_main), ")"),
    label = "tab:SUR_interactions",
    column.labels = c("Sardine", "Anchoveta", "Jack mackerel"),
    column.separate = c(1, 1, 1),
    dep.var.labels.include = FALSE,
    covariate.labels = c(
      "Own biomass ($x_{it}$)",
      "Own biomass$^2$ ($x_{it}^2$)",
      "SST",
      "SST$^2$",
      "Chlorophyll-a",
      "Sardine $\\times$ Anchoveta",
      "Sardine $\\times$ Jack mackerel",
      "Anchoveta $\\times$ Jack mackerel",
      "Constant"
    ),
    se = list(res_rob3_r[[1]]$se, res_rob3_r[[2]]$se, res_rob3_r[[3]]$se),
    digits = 3,
    single.row = TRUE,
    omit.stat = c("f"),
    no.space = TRUE,
    font.size = "footnotesize",
    notes = c("$^{*}$p$<$0.1; $^{**}$p$<$0.05; $^{***}$p$<$0.01. Robust SEs.",
              "Main specification augmented with pairwise biomass interactions.",
              "No interaction term is individually or jointly significant."),
    notes.append = FALSE,
    notes.align = "l"
  )
)
cat(fix_stargazer_notes(tex_rob3), sep = "\n")

```


```{r appendix-predictive, child='paper1/sections/appendix_predictive_diagnostics.Rmd'}
```
